attributes.stem:
A four-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parent due to concerns about his unsteady gait, which has been progressively worsening over the past year. He started walking at 12 months of age but now frequently stumbles and falls. His parent has also noticed small red spots appearing on his cheeks and ears. He has had three chest infections in the past six months requiring antibiotics. He has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is marked truncal and limb ataxia with an unsteady gait. Small, spider-like blood vessels are visible on his cheeks, ears and conjunctivae. Neurological examination reveals jerky eye movements.

Blood tests reveal:

  • Alpha-fetoprotein: 95 µg/L (<5)
  • Immunoglobulin A: 0.2 g/L (0.5-2.5)
  • Immunoglobulin G: 4.5 g/L (5.0-13.0)
  • CD4+ T cells: 200 cells/µL (500-2400)
  • CD8+ T cells: 150 cells/µL (300-1600)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with the classic triad of progressive cerebellar ataxia, oculocutaneous telangiectasia, and recurrent infections, which strongly suggests ataxia telangiectasia (AT). The elevated alpha-fetoprotein, low immunoglobulin levels, and T-cell lymphopenia support this diagnosis.

Ataxia telangiectasia is an autosomal recessive disorder due to mutations in the ATM gene. The condition typically presents in early childhood with progressive cerebellar ataxia, oculocutaneous telangiectasia, recurrent infections due to combined B and T cell immunodeficiency, and elevated alpha-fetoprotein levels.

Duchenne muscular dystrophy presents with progressive proximal muscle weakness, particularly affecting the legs, with characteristic pseudohypertrophy of calf muscles. It does not cause telangiectasia or immunodeficiency.

Selective IgA deficiency is characterised by isolated low or absent IgA with normal levels of other immunoglobulins. While it can cause recurrent infections, it does not cause neurological symptoms or telangiectasia.

Friedreich ataxia typically presents later in childhood with progressive ataxia and areflexia but does not feature telangiectasia or immunodeficiency. Alpha-fetoprotein levels are normal.

Fragile X syndrome presents with intellectual disability, behavioural problems, and characteristic facial features, including a long face and prominent ears. It does not cause telangiectasia or immunodeficiency.

The management of ataxia telangiectasia involves supportive care, including physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy. Regular monitoring for infections and malignancies is essential due to the associated immunodeficiency and cancer predisposition.
learningPoints: Ataxia telangiectasia typically presents in early childhood with progressive cerebellar ataxia and oculocutaneous telangiectasia.
Alpha-fetoprotein is characteristically elevated in ataxia telangiectasia.
Patients with ataxia telangiectasia have increased susceptibility to infections and malignancies due to combined B and T cell immunodeficiency.
references: Amirifar P, Ranjouri MR, Yazdani R, Abolhassani H, Aghamohammadi A. Ataxia-telangiectasia: A review of clinical features and molecular pathology. Pediatr Allergy Immunol. 2019;30(3):277-288. doi:10.1111/pai.13020
Lee AY, Gray PE. Evaluating for immunodeficiency in children with recurrent infection. Aust Fam Physician. 2014;43(9):629-632.
McCusker C, Upton J, Warrington R. Primary immunodeficiency. Allergy Asthma Clin Immunol. 2018;14(Suppl 2):61. Published 2018 Sep 12. doi:10.1186/s13223-018-0290-5
